-l
Fern Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street . Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4-232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_0886
May I l,2OO9
Thomas Vallance
Senior Staff Counsel
Department of Conservation
801 K Srreet, MS-24-03
Sacramento, CA 95814
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-096
Dear Mr. Vallance:
This letter is intesponse to your request regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the ..Act,'). I
QUESTTONS
l ' May the employees of the Division of oil, Gas, and Geothermal Resources in
the Department of conservation accept transportation to and from offshore oii prutror-.
from the operators to perform state regulatory Autiesi 
-
- 
2' May the employees of the Division of oil, Gas, and Geothermal Resources in
the Department of Conservation accept food, Iodgirg oo off t ore oil prutior-, rrom the
operators when performing state regulatory dutiei?
CONCLUSION
Under the facts- you have presented, the payments in question do not meet the
Act's definition of "gift."
I rhe PoliticalReform Act is contained in Government Code Secrions g1000 through gl0l4. All
statutory references are to the Government code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices commission are contained in secrions r8r r0;il;;i 
tr1sgl of ritle 2 of the catifornia
code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division'6 0f the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-09-096
Page No. 2
FACTS
The Division of Oil, Gas, and Geothermal Resources in the Department of
Conservation (the Division) oversees the drilling, operation, maintenance, and plugging
and abandonment of, oil, natural gas, and geothermal wells. There are over 30 offiliorJ
oil platforms and man-made islands in State and Federal waters off the coast of
California. Most of the offshore oil production sites are several nautical miles out to sea.
Facilities within three nautical miles of shore, roughly one-third of the total, are in State
jurisdiction. Those beyond three nautical miles are in Federal Jurisdiction
These offshore facilities are only accessible by boat or helicopter. The facility
operators typically contract with other companies to irovide transportation to and from
the facilities. You stated that you believe the transportation to *d from the facilities is
provided without charge in the regular course of business without regard to official status.
Like anyone else going to and from the offshore facilities, Division fersonnel travel to
and from the sites using the free shuttle service provided by the facility operator. you
note that no viable alternative method of transportation is available.
The Division has about 120 employees, including over 60 professional geologists
and petroleum engineers. Division personnel regularly perform regulatory activities at
offshore oil facilities within State jurisdiction, including-, but not timitea tb, facility
inspections and equipment testing. On occasion, Division staff are required to renain at
the offshore facility fol an extended period, sometimes due to malfunclions in equipment
to be tested and sometimes due to weather conditions. Under these circumstances,lhe
facility operators may provide food and sleeping quarters for Division staff under the
same conditions as are available to the facility crewmembers. Division staff performing
these duties are required to file statements of economic interests (Form 700s).
ANALYSIS
The Act does not impose any restrictions on a state agency that would prohibit it
from accepting payments from outside sources unless those payments are used to confer a
gift on any public officials within the agency. The Act prohibiis members of a state
board or commission and designated employees of a state or local govemment agency
from accepting gifts from any single source in any calendar y"a. *ith a total value of
more than $420 it the member or employee would be required to report the receipt of the
gift from that source on his or her statement of economiC interests. lSection ggsbl(c),
regulation 18940.2.)
Section 82028 (a) defines "gift" as "any payment that confers a personal benefit
on the recipient to the extent that consideration of equal or greater valuJ is not received
and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything ofvalue unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without
regard to official status."


File No. A-09-096
Page No. 3
You have indicated that the expenses the payments are intended to subsidize
include the cost of transportation to the oil platforms and food and lodging when
necessary for the employee to complete his or her work on the platfoni. "
Transportation and accommodations can be considered to confer a personal
benefit on the recipient and, therefore, ordinarily would be considered gifts.
- 
Transportation: Under the facts you have provided, access to the platforms is
limited, thereby necessitating the use of boats or hilicopters. Moreourr, ui""., to the
platforms is essential in fulfilling the employee's duties. While we have generally found
that travel is presumed to confer some personal benefit on a public officia'i (GaultAdvice
Letter' No. 4-07-158), we do not find a personal benefit undir the factual "ir"u-rt*"",
you have described.2 As described, the transportation will be provided to the employees
solely for the purpose of conducting their official duties. Assuming the travel is not
extravagant or lavish, it would be difficult to conclude that the traniportation provided to
a public official on an oil-drilling platform in order to perform his or her duties on that
platform would be considered a "personal benefit." Bicause the payment does not confer
a personal benefit, they are not gifts.
Food and accommodations: The similar reasoning may be applied to the food and
accommodations. You noted that food and accommodati6ns on tfre piatform might be
necessary due to weather conditions that could prevent anyone fromleing able to leave
the platform. Again, under such exigent circumstancm *i assuming the-food and
accommodations are not extravagant or lavish, we conclude there is io personal benefit
and these payments are not considered to be gifts.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
l
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
W. Wallace
General Counsel
Division
I:\Adviceltrs\06-090
2 we reached a similar conclusion in the san Advice Letter, No. 4-09-004.
